title: Amos
author: Amos of Tekoa — a shepherd and dresser of sycamore figs, not a professional prophet
date: ~760–750 BC (during the reign of Jeroboam II of Israel and Uzziah of Judah)
setting: A period of prosperity in the northern kingdom of Israel, masking deep social injustice and religious formalism. Amos comes from Judah to preach against the complacent northern elite.
purpose: To announce God's judgment on the nations and especially on Israel for social oppression, idolatry, and the corruption of justice — and to call the nation back to covenant faithfulness.
themes: God's universal moral judgment on all nations
Social justice: the oppression of the poor
Empty religion vs. true covenant faithfulness
The Day of the LORD as darkness, not light
The plumb line of God's righteous standard

before.label: Elijah and Elisha — prophetic opposition to royal apostasy
Jeroboam II's long and prosperous reign in Israel
Social stratification and exploitation of the rural poor grow

after.label: Hosea — covenant faithlessness and God's pursuing love
Fall of Samaria — Israel's judgment arrives
James cites Acts 15 (Amos 9:11) — Gentiles included in David's tent

key_people.name: Amos of Tekoa
key_people.role: A shepherd and sycamore-fig farmer from Judah called to prophesy in the northern kingdom; his outsider status and rustic origin are part of his prophetic authority against the religious establishment

context: Amos prophesied in the northern kingdom during the reign of Jeroboam II (793–753 BC), a period of unprecedented peace, expansion, and prosperity in Israel. The relative absence of Assyrian pressure and successful military campaigns had produced a wealthy upper class whose prosperity coexisted with severe exploitation of the poor. Amos's message is that covenant prosperity must produce justice, and that liturgical fervour disconnected from justice is abhorrent to God.
christ_connection: James quotes Amos 9:11–12 (LXX) in Acts 15:16–17 as the scriptural basis for accepting Gentiles into the covenant community without circumcision: the 'fallen tent of David' is rebuilt in the resurrection of Jesus, and all nations who bear the Lord's name are included. This is the NT's most direct Amos-fulfilment citation, establishing that the restoration Amos promised is fulfilled in the multiethnic church. Amos's vision of justice 'rolling down like waters' (5:24) is the OT's clearest statement of the social dimension of the kingdom that Jesus inaugurates.

key_verses.note: 'You only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities' — election intensifies accountability rather than exempting from it
'But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream' — the most famous verse in Amos; Israel's elaborate religious life is worthless without matching justice
'In that day I will raise up the booth of David that is fallen' — the restoration promise cited in Acts 15 as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection and the Gentile mission

themes_detail.title: Covenant Privilege and Covenant Accountability
themes_detail.text: Amos opens with oracles against the surrounding nations (1:3–2:3), lulling his audience into agreement before turning his sharpest indictment on Israel and Judah (2:4–16). The pivotal theological move is 3:2: 'You only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you.' Election is not a trump card that nullifies accountability but the very basis for heightened accountability. Being the people of God means being more responsible, not less, for how one treats the poor and upholds justice.

themes_detail.title: Liturgy Without Justice is Abhorrent
themes_detail.text: Amos 5:21–24 is one of the most radical passages in the OT: God says he hates Israel's feasts, despises their solemn assemblies, refuses to accept their offerings, and will not listen to their music. This is not an anti-ritual manifesto but a diagnosis of corrupted worship: when liturgy is performed by people who exploit the poor outside the temple gates, the worship itself becomes an insult. The solemn assemblies are not the alternative to justice but the place where God expects justice to be cultivated and formed.

themes_detail.title: The Day of the LORD Reversed
themes_detail.text: Amos's most shocking theological move is his reversal of popular eschatology: Israel was expecting the Day of the LORD as a day of vindication against its enemies. Amos tells them it will be darkness for them, not light (5:18–20). This is not pessimism but covenant logic: the Day of the LORD is the day when YHWH fully enacts his character — which means it will expose and judge precisely the covenant unfaithfulness that Israel's prosperity has masked. The Day of the LORD is always both judgment and salvation; who experiences which depends entirely on covenant faithfulness.

themes_detail.title: Restoration Beyond Judgment
themes_detail.text: Amos ends unexpectedly (9:11–15) with a vision of restoration: the fallen booth of David rebuilt, the nations included, the land overflowing with agricultural abundance. This is not a contradiction of everything that came before but its resolution: judgment is not God's final word, but it is his necessary word before restoration. The restoration Amos promises — a Davidic king, the nations coming in, a creation that cannot contain its own blessing — is the eschatological vision that the NT identifies as beginning in Jesus' resurrection.
